cards.text: What are the key features of an autosomal dominant pedigree?
cards.answer: Vertical transmission (every generation affected), males and females equally affected, 50% risk to offspring, unaffected individuals do not transmit/vertical transmission, equal sex ratio, 50% risk

tip.tip: Think of Marfan syndrome or achondroplasia

cards.text: What is incomplete penetrance? Give an example.
cards.answer: Some individuals with the disease genotype do not show the phenotype; e.g., retinoblastoma (RB1) has approximately 90% penetrance/carrying the allele without expressing the phenotype

tip.tip: Makes it appear the trait skips a generation

cards.text: What is variable expressivity? Give an example.
cards.answer: Affected individuals show different severities of the same condition; e.g., NF1 — some have only cafe-au-lait spots, others have extensive neurofibromas/different severity in individuals with the same mutation

tip.tip: Different from penetrance which is all-or-nothing

cards.text: What is the recurrence risk when both parents are carriers of an autosomal recessive condition?
cards.answer: 25% affected, 50% carriers, 25% unaffected homozygous normal/25 percent or 1 in 4

tip.tip: Use a Punnett square: Aa x Aa

cards.text: Why are consanguineous marriages associated with higher risk of autosomal recessive disorders?
cards.answer: Related parents are more likely to both carry the same rare recessive allele inherited from a common ancestor/shared rare alleles from common ancestor

tip.tip: 11% consanguinity rate in India, higher in southern states

cards.text: What is the inheritance pattern of mitochondrial disorders?
cards.answer: Maternal inheritance only — mitochondria are inherited exclusively from the mother through the ovum cytoplasm; affected mothers transmit to all children/maternal inheritance
Traits determined by the combined effect of multiple genes (polygenic) plus environmental factors; examples include diabetes, hypertension, neural tube defects/polygenic plus environmental factors

tip.tip: Recurrence risk is empirically determined, not Mendelian ratios

cards.text: What is the genetic basis of achondroplasia?
cards.answer: Autosomal dominant mutation in FGFR3 gene (fibroblast growth factor receptor 3); most cases are de novo mutations from unaffected parents/FGFR3 gain-of-function mutation

tip.tip: Most common cause of short-limbed dwarfism
